question: Compare the Ch.01 site layout (Figure 1.2-1) with the Ch.05 module component diagram (Figure 5.1-1) to show the scale relationship between plant and module.

nuscale_ch05_0003.title: Design Basis
nuscale_ch05_0003.summary: The Design Basis section outlines that the NuScale reactor coolant system (RCS) uses natural circulation without pumps or external piping, ensuring safe and efficient heat transfer from the reactor core to the steam generator. The RCS is designed to maintain proper coolant flow, water chemistry, and boron concentration to protect fuel integrity and support safe operation under normal and transient conditions.

nuscale_ch01_0016.title: Principal Site Characteristics
nuscale_ch01_0016.summary: This section provides a general overview of the NuScale plant design, outlining key systems such as safety features, power conversion, fuel handling, and waste management. It explains that each Combined License (COL) applicant must reference the NuScale Final Safety Analysis Report (FSAR) and supplement it with site-specific information, while distinguishing between certified design elements and conceptual design information (CDI). Security-related details are specially marked and withheld according to regulatory requirements.

nuscale_ch01_0015.title: General Plant Description
nuscale_ch01_0015.summary: This section provides an overview of the NuScale plant design, summarizing key systems such as safety features, power conversion, fuel handling, cooling, waste management, and auxiliary systems. It explains the distinction between certified and site-specific design elements, noting that some information is representative or conceptual and marked accordingly, while security-related details are withheld. Each COL applicant must reference the NuScale FSAR and provide site-specific information as required.

nuscale_ch05_0004.title: System Description
nuscale_ch05_0004.summary: The Reactor Coolant System (RCS) in the NuScale design is located inside the containment vessel and includes key components such as the reactor pressure vessel, pressurizer, and associated piping and controls. The RCS removes heat from the reactor core by natural circulation and interfaces with several other systems for safety and operational control, including degasification and emergency core cooling. Its pressure-retaining parts help maintain the integrity of the reactor coolant pressure boundary, serving as a barrier to radionuclide release.

nuscale_ch01_0060.title: NuScale Integral System Test Program
nuscale_ch01_0060.summary: The NuScale Integral System Test Program was established to collect thermal-hydraulic data for system characterization and to validate safety analysis codes using a scaled model of the NuScale Power Module (NPM) design. The NIST-1 facility replicates key reactor systems and supports the development and validation of proprietary safety codes, ensuring accurate modeling of the NPM’s unique features. Testing at NIST-1 has provided essential data for NuScale’s safety analysis and design certification efforts.

nuscale_ch01_0073.title: Departures
nuscale_ch01_0073.summary: This section outlines the interface requirements between the NuScale Power Plant’s certified design and the site-specific design provided in the combined license (COL) application. It distinguishes between conceptual design information (CDI) for non-certified plant portions and COL information items, which are site-specific responsibilities of the COL applicant. Additionally, it requires COL applicants to provide a list of any departures from the certified design.

nuscale_ch01_0390.title: SRP 9.5.1.1, Rev 0: Fire Protection ProgramII.7 Operational Program and Proposed Implementation Milestones
nuscale_ch01_0390.summary: This section explains that the operational fire protection program and its implementation milestones are the responsibility of the Combined License (COL) applicant, not the NuScale design certification applicant. NuScale acknowledges the relevant regulatory guidance but notes that full conformance is not required at the design certification stage; instead, COL applicants must ensure compliance during their application process.

nuscale_ch01_0068.title: Electrical and Instrumentation and Control Drawings
nuscale_ch01_0068.summary: This section explains that the NuScale Final Safety Analysis Report includes simplified electrical, instrumentation, and control (I&C) drawings to visually clarify design information alongside the text. It lists the types of diagrams provided, references tables and figures for diagram legends, and notes that applicants referencing the NuScale design must supply site-specific diagrams and legends as needed.

nuscale_ch01_0391.title: SRP 9.5.1.2, Rev 0: Risk-Informed, Performance-Based Fire Protection ProgramAll Various
nuscale_ch01_0391.summary: This section explains that the development and implementation of a risk-informed, performance-based fire protection program (SRP 9.5.1.2, Rev 0) is not applicable to the NuScale design certification application, as it is the responsibility of Combined License (COL) applicants who reference the NuScale design. The NuScale application addresses fire protection criteria only to the extent necessary to enable COL applicants to meet regulatory requirements.

nuscale_ch01_0033.title: General Arrangement of Major Structures and Equipment
nuscale_ch01_0033.summary: This section describes the general arrangement of major structures and equipment at the NuScale Power Plant, focusing on the central Reactor Building (RXB). The RXB is a large, reinforced concrete structure designed for safety and radiation protection, housing the NuScale Power Modules (NPMs) in a common reactor pool with individual bays arranged in two rows. The section also references figures for detailed layouts and notes that some buildings, like administration and warehouse facilities, are outside the scope of this report.

nuscale_ch01_0036.title: Refueling Operations
nuscale_ch01_0036.summary: Refueling operations for a NuScale Power Module (NPM) are conducted independently from other modules. The process involves moving the NPM from its operating bay to the refueling pool, disassembling it using cranes and flange tools for inspection, maintenance, and refueling, and then reassembling and returning it to operation after successful testing.

nuscale_ch01_0055.title: NuScale Testing Programs
nuscale_ch01_0055.summary: This section outlines the testing programs conducted to verify and confirm the unique design features of the NuScale Power Module, particularly where prior data or operational experience was lacking. It details completed and ongoing tests related to fuel design, steam generator, control rod assemblies, and integrated system phenomena, with a focus on supporting safety analyses and licensing requirements. Specifically, it describes critical heat flux (CHF) testing for both preliminary and final fuel designs to support code development and safety analysis.

nuscale_ch01_0054.title: Requirements for Additional Technical Information
nuscale_ch01_0054.summary: This section outlines the verification and confirmation testing programs for unique NuScale Power Module (NPM) design features that support the plant’s safety analysis. It details completed and ongoing tests, particularly those for the fuel design, steam generator, control rod assemblies, and integrated system phenomena, emphasizing the need for new data where prior experience was lacking. Specifically, it describes critical heat flux (CHF) testing for the preliminary fuel design, which informed code development and safety analysis for licensing.

nuscale_ch01_0037.title: Control Building
nuscale_ch01_0037.summary: The section describes the refueling operations for a NuScale Power Module (NPM), which are conducted independently for each module. The process involves moving the NPM from its operating bay to the refueling area using specialized cranes and tools, disassembling it for inspection, maintenance, and refueling, and then reassembling and returning it to service after successful testing.

nuscale_ch01_0051.title: Plant Features of Special Interest
nuscale_ch01_0051.summary: This section describes key plant features of special interest at the NuScale Power Plant, including the Central Utility Building (CUB) and Diesel Generator Buildings (DGBs), which house essential but nonsafety-related equipment and services. It also highlights human factors considerations in the plant’s design, such as fail-safe functionality, centralized multi-module control, digital interfaces, and workload management, all aimed at minimizing human error and meeting regulatory requirements.

nuscale_ch01_0000.title: Preface
nuscale_ch01_0000.summary: The Preface outlines the copyright and usage restrictions for the NuScale Standard Plant Design Certification Application, specifying that only the U.S. Nuclear Regulatory Commission (NRC) is authorized to use, copy, or disclose the document as needed for regulatory purposes. It also notes that proprietary information is protected, and any copies made by the NRC must include the appropriate copyright and proprietary notices.

edges.description: Section A provides a detailed description of the reactor coolant system within a single module, while Section B gives a general overview of the entire plant layout, allowing for a comparison of the module's scale relative to the overall facility.

Section A provides a detailed description of the reactor coolant system within the module, while Section B gives an overview of the entire plant layout, allowing for a comparison of the module's scale relative to the overall facility.

Section A provides a general overview of the reactor coolant system as a subsystem of the NuScale Power Module, while Section B specifies detailed functions and components of the RCS described in Section A, such as degasification and pressure boundary integrity.

Section A describes the reactor coolant system as a subsystem of the NuScale Power Module, while Section B discusses a scaled test facility that replicates the integrated phenomena of the module, connecting the physical design (A) to its scaled representation (B) for validation.

Section A describes the internal components and scale of the reactor module (NPM), while Section B references the overall plant layout and boundaries, allowing a comparison of module size within the context of the entire plant as depicted in their respective figures.

Section A provides a general overview of the entire plant layout, while Section B details specific module components, allowing a comparison of their respective scales by connecting the overall facility context with individual module design.

Section A provides a general description of the overall plant layout and systems, while Section B describes a scaled test program that models the module design, thus together they allow comparison of the full plant's scale (from A) to the module's scale (from B) as requested in the analysis question.

Section A provides a general description of the overall plant facility, while Section B references Section A (specifically Section 1.2 and Figure 1.2-1) to identify and delineate the certified design boundaries within the site layout, thus connecting the plant-scale overview to module/component-level details.

Section A provides a general overview of the entire plant layout and systems, while Section B details specific module-level components (such as the Reactor Coolant System), allowing a comparison of their respective scales and illustrating how individual modules fit within the overall plant described in Section A.

Section A provides a general description of the overall plant layout and systems, while Section B describes a scaled test program that models the module design, thus together they enable a comparison of the physical scale between the full plant and its module components.

Section A provides a general description of the overall plant layout, while Section B references Section A and specifically identifies Figure 1.2-1 as the representation of the overall facility, thereby connecting the general plant description to the certified design interfaces.

Section B describes a scaled test facility (NIST-1) that replicates the integrated thermal-hydraulic phenomena of the module described in Section A, thereby providing empirical data to validate the system described in A and supporting the comparison of scale between the plant and module layouts.

Section B references Figure 1.2-1 to depict the overall facility layout, while Section A describes module-level system components shown in Figure 5.1-1, thus Section A provides detailed module information that specifies the general plant layout context from Section B, enabling a scale comparison between plant and module.

Section B references Figure 1.2-1, which provides the overall facility layout needed to compare with the module component diagram from Ch.05, thus connecting the plant-scale site layout to the module scale for the analysis question.

Section A provides detailed component-level drawings that specify the layout and arrangement of electrical and I&C systems, which give concrete details to the general plant layout and system descriptions summarized in Section B.

Section A references regulatory criteria and guidance applicable to the overall plant design, including site layout, while Section B provides a summary description of the reactor coolant system as a subsystem within the module, thus Section B specifies technical details that relate to the broader plant context described in Section A.

Section A specifies regulatory fire protection program criteria that must be addressed in the plant's design, while Section B provides the general plant description and layout that must conform to such regulatory requirements, connecting them through the need for the site layout and module arrangement to satisfy fire protection criteria.

Section A references regulatory criteria for fire protection programs that must be implemented in the plant design, while Section B provides the general plant description that would include the physical layout and systems subject to those fire protection requirements, thus Section B's content is necessary context for applying Section A's criteria.

Section A provides the overall plant layout (including Figure 1.2-2), while Section B describes the internal arrangement of the reactor module (detailed in Figure 5.1-1), allowing a direct comparison of the physical scale between the entire plant and an individual module as requested by the analysis question.

Section A provides a detailed layout of major plant structures (including the site layout in Figure 1.2-2), while Section B offers a general description of the overall facility, establishing that the site layout in A specifies the physical arrangement for the general plant description in B.

Section A provides a detailed layout of major plant structures (including a figure of the site layout), while Section B offers a general description of the plant and its systems, with Section A specifying the physical arrangement that illustrates the broader descriptions in Section B.

Section A establishes the regulatory context and requirements for fire protection programs, while Section B provides the detailed drawings (including site and module diagrams) that visually represent the plant and module layouts, allowing for a comparison of their scale as required by the analysis question.

Section A and Section B both address fire protection program requirements and their applicability to NuScale's design certification, with Section B referencing the same regulatory guidance as Section A but focusing on a risk-informed, performance-based approach.

Section A describes the physical movement and spatial requirements for refueling operations involving the NuScale Power Module (NPM), which directly relates to the scale and arrangement of plant and module components depicted in the site layout and module component diagrams referenced in the analysis question.

Sections A and B are identical excerpts describing the refueling operations process, which is relevant for understanding how module movement (from the module diagram) fits within the overall plant layout (site layout), thus connecting the operational flow between plant and module scales.

Section A describes testing programs for unique design features supporting safety analysis, while Section B details operational procedures for moving reactor modules within the plant, both referencing the physical configuration and handling of NuScale Power Modules, thus connecting through the design and operational context of plant layout and module arrangement.

Section A outlines the need for verification and confirmation tests of unique design features supporting the safety analysis, while Section B describes specific plant structures and their functions, both providing context for understanding the physical layout and scale of plant components in relation to the overall site layout and module diagrams.
